elements.sent-2411-001: Xiaolong Xu,~\IEEEmembership{Senior Member,~IEEE, Min Liu,~\IEEEmembership{Senior Member,~IEEE, Muhammad Bilal,~\IEEEmembership{Senior Member,~IEEE, Yuwei Wang,~\IEEEmembership{Member,~IEEE, Xujing Li, and Yu Zheng,~\IEEEmembership{Fellow,~IEEE \markboth{REFOL: Resource-Efficient Federated Online Learning for Traffic Flow Forecasting {Shell \MakeLowercase{et al. : A Sample Article Using IEEEtran.cls for IEEE Journals.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: Multiple federated learning (FL) methods are proposed for traffic flow forecasting (TFF) to avoid heavy-transmission and privacy-leaking concerns resulting from the disclosure of raw data in centralized methods.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, these FL methods adopt offline learning which may yield subpar performance, when concept drift occurs, i.e., distributions of historical and future data vary.
elements.sent-2411-004: Online learning can detect concept drift during model training, thus more applicable to TFF.
elements.sent-2411-005: Nevertheless, the existing federated online learning method for TFF fails to efficiently solve the concept drift problem and causes tremendous computing and communication overhead.
elements.sent-2411-006: Therefore, we propose a novel method named Resource-Efficient Federated Online Learning (REFOL) for TFF, which guarantees prediction performance in a communication-lightweight and computation-efficient way.
elements.sent-2411-007: Specifically, we design a data-driven client participation mechanism to detect the occurrence of concept drift and determine clients' participation necessity.
elements.sent-2411-008: Subsequently, we propose an adaptive online optimization strategy, which guarantees prediction performance and meanwhile avoids meaningless model updates.
elements.sent-2411-009: Then, a graph convolution-based model aggregation mechanism is designed, aiming to assess participants’ contribution based on spatial correlation without importing extra communication and computing consumption on clients.
elements.sent-2411-010: Finally, we conduct extensive experiments on real-world datasets to demonstrate the superiority of REFOL in terms of prediction improvement and resource economization.
elements.sent-2411-011: It is forecast that drivers will expend \$480 \textcolor{black {billion for time delay, fuel waste, and carbon emission caused by traffic congestion in the top 25 crowded cities of U.S.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: For alleviating traffic jams, traffic flow forecasting (TFF) is regarded as the foremost component, based on which the administration is capable of foreseeing the burst flow and then taking measures accordingly\cite{guo2019attention .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Therefore, TFF has brought about extensive interests both in academia and industry, with great quantity of traffic nodes (i.e., sensors, loop detectors and radar-video integrated machines) deployed along roads generating enormous real-time traffic data \cite{miglani2019deep and large variety of innovative methods proposed to enhance performance gains in TFF.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Nowadays, most TFF methods rely on powerful deep neural networks to improve prediction performance, with Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)\cite{8894895 adopted to capture temporal patterns inside traffic flows and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) \cite{liang2019urbanfm as well as Graph Neural Networks (GNN) \cite{zheng2020gman used for evaluating spatial correlation among traffic nodes to boost performance.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, in these centralized methods, large volume of historical traffic data generated by traffic nodes should be transmitted to a central server for training prediction models, resulting in considerable transmission overhead.
elements.sent-2411-016: Federated learning (FL) emerges as a potent solution to tackle these problems above \cite{yang2019federated , where traffic nodes (termed clients) are orchestrated to collaboratively train a global prediction model without disclosure of raw data.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Some recent researches have focused on forecasting traffic flow in FL architecture \cite{liu2020privacy, zhang2021fastgnn, meng2021cross .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In \cite{meng2021cross , clients adopt encoder-decoder architecture of Gated Recurrent Units network (GRU) to model temporal patterns, and the central server uses GNN for updating encoders' hidden states to evaluate spatial correlation among traffic nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, all of these proposed methods adopt the offline learning manner where prediction models are firstly trained based on historical traffic data and then deployed at traffic nodes for future traffic forecasting.
elements.sent-2411-020: When {concept drift occurs, i.e., the distribution of historical traffic data and the newly-detected traffic data changes \cite{lu2018learning , directly applying these pre-trained models to the new traffic data may easily yield unsatisfactory prediction performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: In contrast, online learning (OL) \cite{hoi2021online makes it possible to detect concept drift upon newly-observed data arriving, and then optimize prediction models accordingly.}
elements.sent-2411-022: With the merits of capturing fluctuating patterns and timely model updates, OL is more applicable for TFF to achieve much more attractive prediction performance.
elements.sent-2411-023: In \cite{10618965 , Liu et al. first propose a Federated Online Learning (FOL) method for traffic flow prediction by integrating OL into FL paradigm.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Specifically, all clients need to update prediction models once they detect new traffic data, regardless of whether concept drift occurs, which results in large amount of computational cost for local optimization and communication cost for exchanging model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-025: Therefore, it is necessary to reasonably detect concept drift for avoiding resource waste derived from client-side ineffective model updates with little contribution to performance promotion.
elements.sent-2411-026: Besides, it is challenging to design a resource-efficient mechanism for evaluating the time-variant spatial correlation resulting from data fluctuation without frequent client-server parameter transmission and extra computational operations on clients, aiming to decrease resource consumption while guaranteeing prediction performance.
elements.sent-2411-027: To this end, we propose a novel TFF method in FOL paradigm named Resource-Efficient Federated Online Learning (REFOL), aiming to reduce computational and communication cost at clients while guaranteeing prediction performance.
elements.sent-2411-028: Specifically, we first design a data-driven client participation mechanism, which enables each client to autonomously detect whether concept drift occurs by calculating data distribution divergence and further determine the necessity of local model updates.
elements.sent-2411-029: Furthermore, clients conduct adaptive online optimization locally to eliminate the influence of concept drift and guarantee performance gains.
elements.sent-2411-030: Finally, instead of simple averaging mechanism, we adopt a novel graph convolution-based model aggregation mechanism to efficiently evaluate the importance of local updated models depending on time-varying spatial correlation among traffic nodes and further yield the fresh global model with superior generalization ability.
elements.sent-2411-031: The main contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-032: We propose a novel method called REFOL to precisely forecast traffic flow with spatio-temporal variation in a computation-efficient and communication-lightweight way in federated online learning (FOL) paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-033: We design a data-driven client participation mechanism and an adaptive online optimization strategy, which makes clients detect concept drift and further determine requisite model updates at the least sacrifice of computing and communication resources.
elements.sent-2411-034: We design a novel graph convolution-based aggregation mechanism to ensure the generalization ability of global models, which can reasonably quantify participants' importance based on spatial correlation evaluation without importing extra resource consumption on traffic nodes.
elements.sent-2411-035: We validate the efficiency and effectiveness of REFOL by conducting comparison experiments with other FL and FOL prediction methods on two real-world datasets.
elements.sent-2411-036: The remainder of this paper is organized as below.
elements.sent-2411-037: Section II presents the literature review on \textcolor{black {TFF , \textcolor{black {FL , and concept drift detection.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: In Section III, we formulate the TFF problem in FOL paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-039: Section IV elaborates the technical details of the proposed REFOL.
elements.sent-2411-040: Then, we analyze the performance of REFOL from experimental results in Section V.
elements.sent-2411-041: Finally, we conclude the paper in Section VI.

elements.sent-2411-060: In FedOSTC, all clients are selected to perform local optimization once they receive new traffic data, in order to mitigate performance decreasing caused by concept drift.
elements.sent-2411-061: However, this method fails to consider practical model updating requirements on clients and results in unnecessarily tremendous communication and computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-062: Concept drift reflects the unforeseeable changes in underlying data distribution over time, and consequentially makes the existing prediction model unable to fit the upcoming data, resulting in poor prediction performance \cite{lu2016concept,lu2018learning .}
elements.sent-2411-063: It is of great importance to accurately detect concept drift, benefiting for timely model update and efficiently preventing performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-064: Concept drift detection methods can be classified into two categories, i.e, pre-prediction methods and after-prediction methods.
elements.sent-2411-065: The former devotes to quantifying the distribution divergence between the historical data and new data by certain divergence metrics before conducting prediction \cite{kifer2004detecting,gu2016concept,bu2017incremental .}
elements.sent-2411-066: The latter first conducts prediction, and then evaluates the prediction errors to judge whether concept drift occurs.

elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{method.pdf} \caption{The architecture of REFOL includes three modules, i.e., {data-driven participation mechanism}, {adaptive online optimization}, and {graph convolution-based model aggregation}. elements.sent-2411-098: Specifically, REFOL is composed of data-driven client participation mechanism, adaptive online optimization, and graph convolution-based model aggregation.

elements.sent-2411-134: Generally, different participants exhibit diverse importance in TFF scenarios due to the coupling spatial correlation.
elements.sent-2411-135: Therefore, it is necessary to govern aggregation weights of participating nodes based on sophisticated spatial correlation for obtaining the updated global model with satisfactory generalization ability.
elements.sent-2411-136: However, existing FL methods evaluate participants' importance by importing an additional assessment procedure of spatial correlation, calling for frequent transmission and thus resulting in tremendous communication overhead.
elements.sent-2411-137: Thus, we design a novel graph convolution-based model aggregation mechanism, the execution process of which is shown in Fig. \ref{aggregation .}
elements.sent-2411-138: It leverages graph convolution to online quantify time-variant spatial correlation among participants and correspondingly yields aggregation weights in a communication-lightweight and computation-efficient manner.
elements.sent-2411-139: The whole procedure is elaborated as follows.
elements.sent-2411-140: Suppose $N_t$ clients participate in the $t$-th round according to the designed data-driven client participation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-141: These participants execute the process of adaptive online optimization and subsequently upload the updated local models to the central server for generating the fresh global model $w_{t+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-142: They can also compose a directed graph, denoted as $\mathcal{G _t=(\mathcal{N _t, \mathcal{E _t)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: As is shown in Fig. \ref{aggregation (a), the purple nodes represent the participants.}

elements.sent-2411-185: In this subsection, the whole procedure of REFOL is elaborated in Algorithm \ref{ag3 .}
elements.sent-2411-186: At the beginning of each round, each client firstly detects concept drift by evaluating distribution difference of traffic data between the current and previous rounds using KLD, followed by comparing with threshold $Q$ to determine whether to download the fresh global model or reuse the locally-saved model for online forecasting using Algorithm \ref{ag2 .}
elements.sent-2411-187: After then the participants upload the updated models to the aggregator for aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-188: The aggregator calculates the aggregation weights of participants via 2-layer graph convolution, and then performs model aggregation to generate the fresh global model (Line 5-8).

elements.sent-2411-189: In this section, comprehensive experiments are conducted to validate the high efficiency of our proposed REFOL.
elements.sent-2411-190: Firstly, we present a brief introduction of the system configurations including datasets, metrics, experiment settings, and baselines.
elements.sent-2411-191: Then we analyze the performance comparisons of REFOL and baselines.
elements.sent-2411-192: Finally, the ablation study is conducted and the effects of varying parameter settings on prediction performance are further explored.
elements.sent-2411-193: The experiment datasets are generated from two real-world datasets: PEMS-BAY and METR-LA \cite{li2018diffusion \footnote{\textcolor{black {The datasets can be downloaded \href{https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/10FOTa6HXPqX8Pf5WRoRwcFnW9BrNZEIX {here .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-194: PEMS-BAY includes the vehicular speed from 325 sensors in the Bay Area from 2017/1/1 to 2017/5/31.
elements.sent-2411-195: METR-LA is comprised of traffic speed collected by 207 sensors in the highway of Los Angeles County from 2012/3/1 to 2012/6/30.
elements.sent-2411-196: The adjacent matrices of sensors are constructed as per \cite{li2018diffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-197: Two widely-used metrics for regression tasks, Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) are adopted to assess the prediction performance.

elements.sent-2411-199: All experiments are conducted on a server with Intel(R) Xeon(R) Gold 5218 CPU @ 2.30GHz and two NVIDIA Geforce RTX 3090 Founders Edition GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-200: We implement REFOL with PyTorch, where a GRU network with 1 layer with 128 hidden cells is used as the prediction model.
elements.sent-2411-201: The learning rate is set to 0.001 and the local epoch $E$ is set to 5.
elements.sent-2411-202: The KLD threshold $Q$ is set to $0.0003$ (how to determine such threshold is analyzed in Section \ref{Q ).}
elements.sent-2411-203: We adopt MSE as the loss function.
elements.sent-2411-204: We divide the traffic flow series into samples by sliding window strategy, as per \cite{meng2021cross .}
elements.sent-2411-205: For batch learning methods, all samples are split to train, validation, and test datasets with the ratio of 7:1:2.
elements.sent-2411-206: For online learning, we average the prediction errors of the samples belonging to the test dataset in the batch learning methods to achieve fair comparison.
elements.sent-2411-207: Note that all of these baselines are trained in offline learning and the configurations of baselines comply with the corresponding literature, unless stated otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-208: The brief introduction of these baselines is presented as follows.
elements.sent-2411-209: SVR (Support Vector Regression) \cite{feng2006svm : Each client independently predicts traffic flow using SVR and doesn't interact with the central server.}
elements.sent-2411-210: STGCN \cite{yu2018spatio : It is centralized method, where the graph convolution and gated temporal convolution are adopted for fast training and accurate performance.. \textcolor{black {.}}}
elements.sent-2411-211: MegaCRN \cite{jiang2023spatio : It is a centralized method, where meta-graph convolutional recurrent network is proposed to tackle the spatio-temporal heterogeneity.. \textcolor{black {.}}}
elements.sent-2411-212: FedAvg \cite{mcmahan2017communication : It is a conventional framework, where the central server aggregates the locally-updated model parameters via averaging mechanism.}
elements.sent-2411-213: The server conducts aggregation by averaging mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-214: The server aggregates the GCN model parameters by averaging mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-215: The server aggregates the adjacency matrix and model parameters across clusters.
elements.sent-2411-216: FedGTP \cite{yang2024fedgtp : It is a novel federated graph-based traffic prediction framework for better capturing intra-client spatial dependencies.. \textcolor{black {.}}}

elements.subsection-2411-011: Comparison of Computational and Communication Cost at Clients

elements.sent-2411-237: We compare the communication and computational cost of all clients in REFOL with the other two FOL prediction methods.
elements.sent-2411-238: These FOL methods are introduced as follows.
elements.sent-2411-239: FedOSTC \cite{10618965 : All clients are selected as participants at each round.}
elements.sent-2411-240: Each client trains an encoder-decoder architecture of GRU locally.
elements.sent-2411-241: The server maintains a GAT for evaluating the spatial correlation and aggregates local models via averaging strategy.
elements.sent-2411-242: FOL-vanilla: The server randomly selects a certain number of clients as participants at each round.
elements.sent-2411-243: For fair comparison, the participant number is equal to the averaged participant number over $\mathcal{T}$ rounds in REFOL.
elements.sent-2411-244: Adaptive online optimization contains two functions, i.e., prediction and local optimization.
elements.sent-2411-245: In fact, these two functions can be fulfilled by the processes of $forward$ $propagation$ and $backward$ $propagation$ in the context of machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-246: Hence, we calculate the computational cost of the two processes.
elements.sent-2411-247: The forward propagation of GRU is shown in Eq. (6)-(9) and we ignore the computational cost generated by Eq. (7).
elements.sent-2411-248: According to \cite{molchanov2019pruning , the computational cost of Eq. (6)-(8) can be calculated as $\left( {1 + hs \right) \times hs \times 3 \times 2 ({\rm FLOPs )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-249: The computational cost of the fully connected layer is $ hs \times 2 ({\rm FLOPs )$.}
elements.sent-2411-250: The computational cost of one forward propagation in GRU is \begin{equation}\left( {1 + hs} \right) \times hs \times 3 \times 2 + hs \times 2 ({\rm FLOPs}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-251: The computational cost of backward propagation is twice as much as that of the forward propagation \cite{epoch2021backwardforwardFLOPratio .}
elements.sent-2411-252: Hence, the computational cost of one backward propagation is \begin{equation}2 \times \left(\left( {1 + hs} \right) \times hs \times 3 \times 2 + hs \times 2 \right) ({\rm FLOPs}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-253: In FedOSTC, the encoder and decoder are both GRUs.
elements.sent-2411-254: Hence, we can calculate the computational cost accordingly as above.
elements.sent-2411-255: Moreover, we consider the computational cost in calculating KLD values.
elements.sent-2411-256: The process can be split into three operations as.
elements.sent-2411-259: Therefore, the computational cost of calculating KLD value is $H\times 7{\rm (FLOPs)}$.
elements.sent-2411-260: Then we dive into how to calculate the communication cost of all clients in different methods.
elements.sent-2411-261: For FOL-vanilla and REFOL, the participants just need to transmit model parameters of the prediction models (i.e., GRU network).
elements.sent-2411-262: Based on Eq. (6)-(9), the model parameter amount of GRU layer can be calculated as$(3 \times hs + 3 \times hs \times hs + 3 \times hs)$.
elements.sent-2411-263: In addition, the parameter amount of the fully connected layer is $(hs + 1)$.
elements.sent-2411-264: Therefore, the parameter amount of prediction model can be calculated as \begin{equation}3 \times hs \times (hs + 2) + hs + 1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-265: Since the encoder and decoder module in FedOSTC are all GRU networks, the model parameter amount can also be calculated accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-266: Furthermore, the additional parameters should be exchanged between clients with the aggregator in FedOSTC, for updating clients' hidden states and optimizing GNN parameters, which can be calculated as follows \cite{meng2021cross : \begin{equation}hs + [3 \times 2hs \times (2hs + 2) + (2hs + 1) ].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-267: The experimental results on prediction performance, computational cost, and communication cost at clients are presented in Table \ref{online_comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-268: It is illustrated that the FOL-vanilla performs worst in terms of perdiction accuracy, in spite of the equal participants number as REFOL.
elements.sent-2411-269: Therefore, the computational and communication costs keep constant on two datasets.
elements.sent-2411-270: While in REFOL, each client need to determine whether to participate in federated round based on traffic distribution, the cost reductions on different datasets vary, resulting variant computational and communication costs on PEMS-BAY and METR-LA datasets.
elements.sent-2411-271: Although FedOSTC achieves the best prediction accuracy, compared with FOL-vanilla and REFOL, the yielded computational cost and communication cost are much higher than the other two methods, mainly because all clients are selected as participants at each training round and extra parameters should be transmitted for updating hidden states and for optimizing GAT's parameters.
elements.sent-2411-272: Compared with FOL-vanilla, REFOL has an extra lightweight process of calculating KLD values at clients.
elements.sent-2411-273: Therefore, the total computational cost of clients in REFOL is little higher than that in FOL-vanilla.
elements.sent-2411-274: But more importantly, REFOL can guarantee prediction performance, with a small drop from the best-performed FedOSTC, while significantly decreasing the computational and communication costs by {$\mathbf{76.56\%}$} and $\mathbf{87.57\%}$ ($\mathbf{41.76\%}$ and $\mathbf{62.52\%}$) on PEMS-BAY dataset (METR-LA dataset) respectively.
elements.sent-2411-275: This improvement results from the designed data-driven client participation mechanism to avoid redundant model updates and graph convolution-based model aggregation to integrate the process of evaluating spatial correlation into model aggregation.

elements.sent-2411-276: In this subsection, we conduct ablation study to verify the effectiveness of different components in REFOL.
elements.sent-2411-277: Firstly, we transform the REFOL method into the following variants.
elements.sent-2411-278: REFOL-D: Each client online performs prediction distributedly.
elements.sent-2411-279: When concept drift occurs, clients independently optimize their locally-owned models.
elements.sent-2411-280: REFOL-V1: The central server randomly selects participants at each round and aggregates local models based on our designed aggregation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-281: We calculate the average number of participants per round in REFOL as the selected number in REFOL-V1.
elements.sent-2411-282: REFOL-V2: It is the same with our designed REFOL except that the server aggregates local models based on the averaging strategy.
elements.sent-2411-283: We conduct extensive experiments on PEMS-BAY dataset to compare the prediction performance of different variants and the predicting results are summarized in Table \ref{ablation .}
elements.sent-2411-284: It is plain that REFOL performs best among all the variants with different forecasting steps.
elements.sent-2411-285: Therefore, the smaller the forecasting step is, the more performance gains REFOL will offer.
elements.sent-2411-286: Specifically, compared with REFOL-D, clients can download the global model from the central server in REFOL, when detecting the occurrence of concept drift.
elements.sent-2411-287: The performance gains in REFOL demonstrate that the global model is superior to local models and the FOL paradigm is more effective than the distributed learning paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-288: Compared with REFOL-V1, REFOL can offer performance gains of 74\% in terms of RMSE, which indicates the high efficiency of data-driven client participation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-289: In REFOL-V2, the central server utilizes averaging aggregation strategy to yield the updated global model without evaluating participating importance of clients.
elements.sent-2411-290: Consequently, global model generalization in REFOL-V2 decreases and the predicting performance declines compared with REFOL, indicating the effectiveness of graph convolution-based aggregation mechanism.

elements.subsection-2411-013: Influence of Q on Prediction Performance
elements.sent-2411-291: In this subsection, \textcolor{black {we analyze how determine the value of $Q$ and explore the influence of $Q$ on prediction performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-292: We first calculate the KLD values between two neighbour traffic sequences for each client, and then calculate the cumulative distribution of these values.
elements.sent-2411-293: The results on PEMS-BAY dataset are shown in Fig. \ref{kl_distribution .}
elements.sent-2411-294: Then, we conduct experiments with 7 different settings of $Q$ (from 0 to 0.0006).
elements.sent-2411-295: Fig. \ref{round (a) and (b) show how RMSE and participation proportion change with $Q$ varying.}
elements.sent-2411-296: We can observe that when $Q$ increases, the concept drift detection criterion is less strict and fewer client participate in federated training (participation proportion declines with $Q$ increasing), leading to poorer performance (RMSE increases with $Q$ increasing).
elements.sent-2411-297: Specifically, the prediction performance of REFOL is consistently superior to that of the baselines, until $Q$ increases to 0.0003.
elements.sent-2411-299: Therefore, the computational cost for local optimization and communication cost for exchanging model parameters can be decreased by up to 72\%.
elements.sent-2411-300: Therefore, to strike a balance between prediction performance and resource consumption, $Q$ is finally determined as 0.0003.
elements.sent-2411-301: Furthermore, we explore the efficiency of data-driven client participation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-302: We randomly choose experimental results of $3$ clients with $F=1$ and $Q=0.0003$ on PEMS-BAY dataset.
elements.sent-2411-303: Fig. \ref{round (c) and (d) show the raw traffic flows and the participation of these three clients respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-304: In Fig. \ref{round (d), when the client downloads the global model at a certain round, the corresponding region is marked as red.}
elements.sent-2411-305: It is intuitive that the participation of clients has much to do with the raw raw traffic flows.
elements.sent-2411-306: Specifically, in the range of the 48-th to 144-th round, there is fluctuation in the traffic flow of $r_1$ and the KLD values are over the given KLD threshold.
elements.sent-2411-307: Therefore, $r_1$ participates and requests the global model from the central server at most rounds in this range.
elements.sent-2411-308: However, the traffic flow of $r_{43}$ is smooth enough, and $r_{43}$ does not detect concept drift and hence reuses the local model for prediction.
elements.sent-2411-309: Therefore, we argue that our proposed data-driven client participation mechanism is efficient in detecting concept drift, and thus enables clients to reasonably determine whether to participate in model updates at each round.

elements.subsection-2411-014: Effect of Graph Convolution Layers on Prediction Performance
elements.sent-2411-310: The number of graph convolution layers determines how well the central server can evaluate spatial dependence among participants and ultimately affects the prediction performance.
elements.sent-2411-311: Hence, in this subsection, we explore the effects of graph convolution layers on prediction performance.
elements.sent-2411-312: The values of RMSE and MAE with different numbers of convolution layers on PEMS-BAY dataset are shown in Fig. \ref{layer .}
elements.sent-2411-313: When the number of convolution layers is $0$, the central server adopts the conventional averaging aggregation mechanism to generate the fresh global model.
elements.sent-2411-314: It is intuitive that our proposed graph convolution-based model aggregation mechanism offers much more performance gains compared with the averaging aggregation mechanism, which demonstrates the effectiveness of our proposed aggregation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-315: Furthermore, with 1-layer graph convolution, the central server fails to evaluate spatial correlation comprehensively, thus yielding subpar prediction performance.
elements.sent-2411-316: With 2-layer graph convolution, the spatial correlation among participants can be well evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-317: When the layer number is greater than 2, the increased layers of graph convolution will pose more computational pressure on the central server but offer few performance improvement.
elements.sent-2411-318: As shown in Fig. \ref{layer , the performance difference with $2$- and $3$-layer graph convolution is negligible.}
elements.sent-2411-319: Overall, the experimental results demonstrate the superiority of our proposed graph convolution-based model aggregation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-320: This reason is that the novel mechanism embraces the assessment of spatial correlation among participants, which can increase global model generalization and further improve prediction performance.
elements.sent-2411-321: In this paper, we investigate the \textcolor{black {TFF problem and propose a novel FOL method named REFOL, aiming at improving prediction performance without bringing out unnecessary computational and communication overhead.}}
elements.sent-2411-322: We design a data-driven client participation mechanism to detect the occurrence of concept drift by evaluating distribution divergence of traffic data.
elements.sent-2411-323: Accordingly, each client decides whether to participate in model updates and further performs adaptive online optimization at each round, which can not only guarantee the prediction performance but also avoid unnecessary computing and communication overhead for insignificant model optimization.
elements.sent-2411-324: Furthermore, we build the immediate evaluation of time-varying spatial correlation in the aggregation process and propose a graph convolution-based model aggregation mechanism, which gets rid of client-side extra resource waste for evaluating spatial dependence like existing FL methods.
elements.sent-2411-325: Finally, comprehensive experiments are conducted on PEMS-BAY and METR-LA datasets to validate the superiority of REFOL in terms of prediction improvement and resource saving.
elements.sent-2411-326: To increase the transparency and reliability of our REFOL, we conduct further discussion as follows.
elements.sent-2411-327: REFOL is oriented for efficiently tackling the concept drift (i.e., traffic distribution shift) for traffic forecasting tasks in FOL paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-328: Different from FedOSTC, where clients need to update local models each round, REFOL enables clients to autonomously detect concept drift and then determine whether to update local models.
elements.sent-2411-329: By this means, clients prefer to reuse the saved historical prediction models for prediction, which can guarantee the prediction performance and simultaneously reduce resource overhead resulting from frequent local update.
elements.sent-2411-330: Moreover, we incorporate the evaluation of spatial correlation into the aggregation process to decrease the communication overhead.
elements.sent-2411-331: Given the ubiquitous distribution drift, we analyze that REFOL can apply to other spatio-temporal forecasting tasks, such as cellular traffic forecasting, weather forecasting, and retail sales forecasting, etc. In different tasks, the KLD threshold $Q$ can adjusted to satisfy specific requirements in prediction accuracy and resource consumption.
elements.sent-2411-332: For example, if we prefer higher prediction accuracy, $Q$ can be set to lower values, which leads to more strict concept drift criteria and clients will update local models more frequently.
elements.sent-2411-333: If we emphasize consumption reduction, $Q$ can be set to higher values, and therefore clients are more likely to reuse the historical model parameters, decreasing the resource consumption.
elements.sent-2411-334: The proposed REFOL can adapt to real-world traffic forecasting scenarios with hundreds or thousands of traffic nodes.
elements.sent-2411-335: Given the complex network conditions in real world, the process of exchanging model parameters between nodes and the central server may be blocked.
elements.sent-2411-336: Therefore, we can supplement waiting time stamps for the central server and nodes.
elements.sent-2411-337: If the time stamp at node expires, it will reuse the locally-saved model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-338: If the time stamp at the central server expires, it will aggregate the punctual model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-339: Moreover, considering that distant nodes have little or no correlation, the topological graph can be split into multiple subgraphs to mitigate the computation load of the central server.
elements.sent-2411-340: We will follow the idea to refine REFOL to accommodate to large-scale traffic nodes in the future works.
elements.sent-2411-342: We assume that all traffic nodes are furnished with similar computing and communication resources.
elements.sent-2411-343: However, traffic nodes may be equipped with imbalanced resources in real world.
elements.sent-2411-344: In such asynchronous FL paradigm, a simple strategy of adapting REFOL may be setting time stamps at the central server and traffic nodes.
elements.sent-2411-345: However, how to guarantee the prediction performance and efficiency is non-trivial.
elements.sent-2411-346: Hence, in the future works, we will dive into asynchronous FL methods for TFF.
